This is why corpus-linguistic methods built for historical language typically rely on rule-based or hybrid parsing architectures, often supplemented by domain-specific grammars or custom training data, rather than statistical next-token prediction. A language model can approximate grammatical structure, but approximation may not always be enough when grammatical nuance is the object of interpretation.

## Exercises

Grammatical part-of-speech analysis has meaningful implications for historical research and for understanding how action and agency are imagined within social and legal contexts.

Extracted triples can show us the dynamics between subjects and objects--the insight needed to understand who or what is described as having agency or as being acted upon. Extracted subject-verb pairs give us a lens to focus on the actions of subjects, and extracted adjective-noun pairs can show us the way in which nouns are characterized by speakers. To strengthen your understanding of how grammatical POS analysis gives us insight into a corpus, do the following exercises: 

# 1) Alter the code above to see the adjectives that modify the nouns "man" and "men" for the year 1879. Do you expect the adjectives to be similar? How might the different adjectives used to modify gendered words reflect the way in which Parliamentarians imagined social order? 

```{R man1879}
library(lubridate)
data("debate_metadata_1870")

hansard_gender_1879 <- hansard_1870 %>%
  left_join(debate_metadata_1870, by = "sentence_id") %>%
  mutate(year = lubridate::year(speechdate)) %>%
  filter(
    year == 1879,
    str_detect(text, regex("\\b(man|men|woman|women)\\b", ignore_case = TRUE))
  )

parsed_1879 <- spacy_parse(
  hansard_gender_1879$text,
  dep = TRUE,
  lemma = FALSE,
  entity = FALSE
)

men_pairs_1879 <- get_adjective_noun_pairs(parsed_1879, c("man", "men")) %>%
  count(adjective_noun_pair, name = "count") %>%
  arrange(desc(count))

women_pairs_1879 <- get_adjective_noun_pairs(parsed_1879, c("woman", "women")) %>%
  count(adjective_noun_pair, name = "count") %>%
  arrange(desc(count))

kable(head(men_pairs_1879, 15), caption = "Top 15 Adjective-Noun Pairs for Men (1879)")
kable(head(women_pairs_1879, 15), caption = "Top 15 Adjective-Noun Pairs for Women (1879)")
```
It is not surprising that the adjectives used for men and women differ, as the most common pairs show some rather clear distinctions. While some general words like “young” and “many” apply to both, most modifiers are specific to each gender. During the 1879 debates, MPs often used terms like `military,` `medical,` and `eminent` to describe men, focusing on their jobs, public roles, and expertise. In contrast, women were usually described by their family status, such as `married` or `unmarried`, or by social and national labels like `Native`, `English`, or `Irish`.

These patterns suggest that Parliament viewed men as active in public and professional life, while women were defined by marriage and social categories. Although these adjective-noun pairs do not represent every parliamentary view, they reveal a clear gender pattern in how social order was described. This example shows that adjective-noun pairs can highlight how nouns are characterized in discourse, and that syntax can reveal broader assumptions about agency, hierarchy, and position, beyond just word frequency.

# 2) We examined gendered adjective-noun pairs for the year 1879. Try changing the year to 1830 instead. How does the way in which Parliamentarians imagine men and women change over time? 

```{r ex2}
data("hansard_1830")
data("debate_metadata_1830")

# 2. Join, filter, and parse for 1830
hansard_gender_1830 <- hansard_1830 %>%
  left_join(debate_metadata_1830, by = "sentence_id") %>%
  mutate(year = lubridate::year(speechdate)) %>%
  filter(
    year == 1830,
    str_detect(text, regex("\\b(man|men|woman|women)\\b", ignore_case = TRUE))
  )

parsed_1830 <- spacy_parse(
  hansard_gender_1830$text,
  dep = TRUE,
  lemma = FALSE,
  entity = FALSE
)

men_pairs_1830 <- get_adjective_noun_pairs(parsed_1830, c("man", "men")) %>%
  count(adjective_noun_pair, name = "count") %>%
  arrange(desc(count))

women_pairs_1830 <- get_adjective_noun_pairs(parsed_1830, c("woman", "women")) %>%
  count(adjective_noun_pair, name = "count") %>%
  arrange(desc(count))

men_comparison <- bind_rows(
  men_pairs_1830 %>% mutate(year = "1830"),
  men_pairs_1879 %>% mutate(year = "1879")
) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(prop = count / sum(count)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(adjective_noun_pair, year, count, prop) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = c(count, prop), values_fill = 0)

women_comparison <- bind_rows(
  women_pairs_1830 %>% mutate(year = "1830"),
  women_pairs_1879 %>% mutate(year = "1879")
) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(prop = count / sum(count)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(adjective_noun_pair, year, count, prop) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = c(count, prop), values_fill = 0)

men_comparison %>%
  mutate(prop_diff = abs(prop_1879 - prop_1830)) %>%
  arrange(desc(prop_diff)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10) %>%
  kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Top 10 Adjective-Noun Pair Comparisons for Men: 1830 vs 1879",
    digits = 3,
    align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r")
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE,
    latex_options = c("hold_position", "scale_down")
  )

women_comparison %>%
  mutate(prop_diff = abs(prop_1879 - prop_1830)) %>%
  arrange(desc(prop_diff)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10) %>%
  kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Top 10 Adjective-Noun Pair Comparisons for Women: 1830 vs 1879",
    digits = 3,
    align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r")
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE,
    latex_options = c("hold_position", "scale_down")
  )


```

Examining adjective-noun pairs over fifty years shows both the similarities and changes in how Parliament described social order and roles. In 1830, men were usually described with broad terms related to rank, class, and public standing, such as `poor,` `great,` and `public.` By 1879, these words appear less often, while more specific terms like `military,` `medical,` and demographic words like “young` become more common. This suggests a shift from describing men mainly in broad status terms to using more specialized and professional roles. 

For women, the biggest change is the use of more detailed marital, legal, and demographic categories. In 1830, women appear less often in the most distinctive adjective-noun pairs, while by 1879, they are described with more specific marital and demographic labels. Because there are few examples of women in 1830, this is best read as a possible shift in emphasis rather than a definite historical change. Still, the later prominence of marital and social classification suggests that Parliament more often described women in terms of legal and social status. Over these fifty years, parliamentary language appears to have become more specific, with men described more by public function and women by legal and social classification.

# 3) Part-of-speech analysis can give us insight into more dimension of a corpus than gender. Filter the debate text for 1860 for the word "future." Can the analysis of the adjectives that modify the noun "future" give us insight into the way in which the future is imagined? Do this exercise again but for the decade 1850. Now explore how William Gladstone describes the future in 1850 versus Benjamin Disraeli. 

```{R future1860}
# Filtering the debate text in the 1860s
library(tidyverse)
library(hansardr)
library(lubridate)
library(spacyr)
library(quanteda)
library(kableExtra)

data("hansard_1860")
data("debate_metadata_1860")

hansard_future_1860 <- hansard_1860 %>%
  left_join(debate_metadata_1860, by = "sentence_id") %>%
  mutate(year = lubridate::year(speechdate)) %>%
  filter(
    year == 1860,
    str_detect(text, regex("\\bfuture\\b", ignore_case = TRUE))
  )

parsed_future_1860 <- spacy_parse(
  hansard_future_1860$text,
  dep = TRUE,
  lemma = FALSE,
  entity = FALSE
)

future_pairs_1860 <- get_adjective_noun_pairs(parsed_future_1860, c("future")) %>%
  count(adjective_noun_pair, name = "count") %>%
  arrange(desc(count))

kable(
  head(future_pairs_1860, 15),
  caption = "Top adjective-noun pairs modifying 'future' in 1860"
)

future_1860_corpus <- corpus(hansard_future_1860, text_field = "text")

future_kwic_1860 <- kwic(
  tokens(future_1860_corpus),
  pattern = "future",
  window = 8,
  case_insensitive = TRUE
)

as_tibble(future_kwic_1860) %>%
  select(pre, keyword, post) %>%
  slice_head(n = 15) %>%
  kbl(
    caption = "KWIC examples for 'future' in 1860",
    col.names = c("Before", "Keyword", "After"),
    booktabs = TRUE
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, position = "left")

```
```{R future1850}
# Filtering the debate text in the 1850s

data("hansard_1850")
data("debate_metadata_1850")

hansard_future_1850 <- hansard_1850 %>%
  left_join(debate_metadata_1850, by = "sentence_id") %>%
  filter(str_detect(text, regex("\\bfuture\\b", ignore_case = TRUE)))

parsed_future_1850 <- spacy_parse(
  hansard_future_1850$text,
  dep = TRUE,
  lemma = FALSE,
  entity = FALSE
)

future_pairs_1850 <- get_adjective_noun_pairs(parsed_future_1850, c("future")) %>%
  count(adjective_noun_pair, name = "count") %>%
  arrange(desc(count))

kable(
  head(future_pairs_1850, 15),
  caption = "Top adjective-noun pairs modifying 'future' in 1850"
)

future_1850_corpus <- corpus(hansard_future_1850, text_field = "text")

future_kwic_1850 <- kwic(
  tokens(future_1850_corpus),
  pattern = "future",
  window = 8,
  case_insensitive = TRUE
)

as_tibble(future_kwic_1850) %>%
  select(pre, keyword, post) %>%
  slice_head(n = 15) %>%
  kbl(
    caption = "KWIC examples for 'future' in 1850",
    col.names = c("Before", "Keyword", "After"),
    booktabs = TRUE
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, position = "left")

```

```{r 1850compare}

# comparing speakers  
data("hansard_1850")
data("speaker_metadata_1850")

future_speakers_1850s <- hansard_1850 %>%
  left_join(speaker_metadata_1850, by = "sentence_id") %>%
  filter(str_detect(text, regex("\\bfuture\\b", ignore_case = TRUE))) %>%
  mutate(
    speaker_group = case_when(
      str_detect(suggested_speaker, regex("gladstone", ignore_case = TRUE)) |
        str_detect(speaker, regex("gladstone", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Gladstone",
      str_detect(suggested_speaker, regex("disraeli", ignore_case = TRUE)) |
        str_detect(speaker, regex("disraeli", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Disraeli",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(speaker_group))

gladstone_text <- future_speakers_1850s %>% filter(speaker_group == "Gladstone")
disraeli_text <- future_speakers_1850s %>% filter(speaker_group == "Disraeli")

parsed_gladstone <- spacy_parse(gladstone_text$text, dep = TRUE, lemma = FALSE, entity = FALSE)
gladstone_pairs <- get_adjective_noun_pairs(parsed_gladstone, c("future")) %>%
  count(adjective_noun_pair, name = "Gladstone_Count")

parsed_disraeli <- spacy_parse(disraeli_text$text, dep = TRUE, lemma = FALSE, entity = FALSE)
disraeli_pairs <- get_adjective_noun_pairs(parsed_disraeli, c("future")) %>%
  count(adjective_noun_pair, name = "Disraeli_Count")

future_speaker_comparison <- full_join(gladstone_pairs, disraeli_pairs, by = "adjective_noun_pair") %>%
  mutate(
    Gladstone_Count = replace_na(Gladstone_Count, 0),
    Disraeli_Count = replace_na(Disraeli_Count, 0)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Gladstone_Count + Disraeli_Count))

kable(
  head(future_speaker_comparison, 15),
  caption = "How Gladstone and Disraeli modify 'future' in the 1850s"
)
```
Looking at how the noun `future` is used shows that Parliament often imagined the future in evaluative and political terms rather than just as a neutral idea of time. In the 1860 debates, however, the adjective-noun pairs attached to the future are sparse: the table shows only `extended future` `extreme future` and `speculative future,` each appearing once. Because the frequency counts are so limited for this single year, they are less revealing on their own; it is better to read them cautiously alongside the KWIC examples rather than as a strong summary of parliamentary language about the future.

Comparing Gladstone and Disraeli in the 1850s suggests a possible difference in how each speaker framed the future, but the evidence here is limited because most of the adjective-noun pairs appear only once. Gladstone’s adjective-noun pairs are limited to `far future` and `near future`, making his language about the future seem more focused on time and more measured. Disraeli, on the other hand, uses a wider and more dramatic range of descriptions, including `Parliamentary future`, `financial future`, `gloomy future`, `terrible future`, and `triumphant future`. These examples suggest that Disraeli may have described the future in more political and evaluative terms, while Gladstone’s language seems more focused on time. However, making a stronger claim would require a closer reading of the surrounding speeches. These adjective-noun pairs do not fully capture each speaker’s complete view of history or politics, but they show that the future was imagined differently by each speaker and in different contexts.

# 4) In our triples analysis example we filter on the subject and objects. Try instead filtering for triples that contain a verb of choice and visualize the results in a table.

Hint: We use the carrot `^` symbol to return items that start with a word and the `$` symbol to return items that end with a word. To search for triples that contain a verb you will need to search for the word as it exists between two spaces. 

```{R triples}
have_triples_1870 <- simple_svo_triples_1870 %>%
  filter(str_detect(svo_triple, "\\bhave\\b")) %>%
  count(svo_triple, sort = TRUE)

have_triples_1870 %>%
  slice_head(n = 15) %>%
  kable(caption = "Top triples containing the verb 'have' in the 1870 women/women subset")
```
When we filter the SVO triples for the verb `have` in the 1870 woman/women subset, we can see which parliamentary subjects were linked to rights, votes, knowledge, or other forms of possession and entitlement. The most common phrases are political and legal, such as `vote` and `right.` Examples include `woman have vote,` `they have right,` and `she have right.` The table also includes other subjects and objects, like `I have doubt,` `House have right,` and `Bill have effect.`

This pattern shows that the verb `have` often anchors debates about rights, suffrage, possession, and legal or political standing. Triples such as `woman have vote` and `woman have right` point directly to discussions of women’s suffrage and legal status, while the broader table shows how parliamentary syntax connects to a range of subjects, including questions of entitlement, knowledge, and institutional effect.


# 5) In the previous chapter we introduced methods of measuring distinctiveness in a corpus using TF-IDF and JSD. Instead of counting top triples, use your knowledge of the application of distinctiveness measurements to determine which adjective noun pairs are distinctive of one year but not another, and vice-versa (e.g. which pairs are distinctive of 1879 versus 1878). Then load the speaker metadata and explore which triples are distinctive across speakers (e.g. which triples are distinctive of William Gladstone versus Benjamin Disraeli).

```{r ex5}
data("hansard_1870")
data("debate_metadata_1870")

gender_terms <- c("man", "men", "woman", "women")

hansard_gender_1878 <- hansard_1870 %>%
  left_join(debate_metadata_1870, by = "sentence_id") %>%
  mutate(year = lubridate::year(speechdate)) %>%
  filter(
    year == 1878,
    str_detect(text, regex("\\b(man|men|woman|women)\\b", ignore_case = TRUE))
  )

hansard_gender_1879 <- hansard_1870 %>%
  left_join(debate_metadata_1870, by = "sentence_id") %>%
  mutate(year = lubridate::year(speechdate)) %>%
  filter(
    year == 1879,
    str_detect(text, regex("\\b(man|men|woman|women)\\b", ignore_case = TRUE))
  )

parsed_1878 <- spacy_parse(
  hansard_gender_1878$text,
  dep = TRUE,
  lemma = FALSE,
  entity = FALSE
)

parsed_1879 <- spacy_parse(
  hansard_gender_1879$text,
  dep = TRUE,
  lemma = FALSE,
  entity = FALSE
)

pairs_1878 <- get_adjective_noun_pairs(parsed_1878, gender_terms) %>%
  count(adjective_noun_pair, name = "n") %>%
  mutate(year = "1878")

pairs_1879 <- get_adjective_noun_pairs(parsed_1879, gender_terms) %>%
  count(adjective_noun_pair, name = "n") %>%
  mutate(year = "1879")

year_pair_counts <- bind_rows(pairs_1878, pairs_1879)

year_pair_tfidf <- year_pair_counts %>%
  bind_tf_idf(adjective_noun_pair, year, n) %>%
  arrange(desc(tf_idf))

year_pair_tfidf %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = tf_idf, n = 15) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  arrange(year, desc(tf_idf)) %>%
  kable(caption = "Adjective-noun pairs distinctive of 1878 versus 1879")
```
```{r ex5pt2}
data("hansard_1850")
data("speaker_metadata_1850")

glad_disraeli_1850 <- hansard_1850 %>%
  left_join(speaker_metadata_1850, by = "sentence_id") %>%
  mutate(
    speaker_group = case_when(
      str_detect(suggested_speaker, regex("gladstone", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Gladstone",
      str_detect(suggested_speaker, regex("disraeli", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Disraeli",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(speaker_group))

parsed_glad_disraeli <- spacy_parse(
  glad_disraeli_1850$text,
  dep = TRUE,
  lemma = TRUE,
  entity = FALSE
)

doc_lookup <- glad_disraeli_1850 %>%
  mutate(doc_id = paste0("text", row_number())) %>%
  select(doc_id, speaker_group)

speaker_triples <- get_simple_lemmatized_triples(parsed_glad_disraeli) %>%
  left_join(doc_lookup, by = "doc_id") %>%
  mutate(svo_triple = str_to_lower(svo_triple)) %>%
  count(speaker_group, svo_triple, sort = TRUE)

speaker_triple_tfidf <- speaker_triples %>%
  bind_tf_idf(svo_triple, speaker_group, n) %>%
  arrange(desc(tf_idf))

speaker_triple_tfidf %>%
  group_by(speaker_group) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = tf_idf, n = 15) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  kable(caption = "Triples distinctive of Gladstone versus Disraeli")
```
Using TF-IDF helps us identify adjective-noun pairs that are especially distinctive in a certain year compared to others. In my analysis, 1878 is characterized by pairs like “Russian men, `Turkish men,` `sensible men,` `moderate men,` and `wealthy man.` These pairs imply that speakers often used national and evaluative labels when describing men. In comparison, pairs from 1879 like `White men,` `sick men,` `wounded man,` `Irish women,` `capable women,` and `loose women` were more common. These examples show how descriptions were based on race, nationality, health, and moral judgment.

After loading speaker metadata and comparing the SVO triples most distinctive of Gladstone and Disraeli, I found that the results were less about policy topics and more about rhetorical style in parliamentary debate. Disraeli’s distinctive triples use first-person, assertive, and procedural phrases like `I tell lord,` `I say it,` `I give notice,` `I avail myself,` and `I pledge myself.` Disraeli’s style seems more declarative and procedural, emphasizing clear statements, stances, and actions. Meanwhile, Gladstone’s triples are more tied to interactions with other members. His phrases include `I hear hon,` `friend tell we,` `he ask hon,` `gentleman raise question,` `I beseech house,` and `you have right.` These show a style based on responding, appealing, and connecting with other members.

Since many of these triples are standard parliamentary expressions, and some are also noisy parsing artifacts, they should not be taken as definitive on their own - but they do show a real difference in how each speaker positioned himself in debate. These results are best used as a starting point for close reading of the sentences around the most distinctive triples before making any stronger historical claim. 

## AI Use statement

**Tool used**: ChatGPT

**Purpose**: I used AI to help debug my `spacyr` setup and troubleshoot why spaCy was not running correctly in R. I also used it to help interpret the source of the error and identify the environment mismatch causing the installation problem.

**Example prompts**: I asked AI why `spacyr` was failing to initialize, what might cause compatibility issues between `spacyr`, `reticulate`, and Python, and how to fix the error once I realized my machine was using Python 3.14 instead of a compatible version.

**Tasks performed independently by me**: I installed the correct Python version, set up the working environment, ran the code myself in R, generated all tables and outputs, checked that the parsing functions worked, chose the years, terms, and speakers I analyzed, and wrote the historical interpretations and conclusions in the assignment myself.

**explanation of code**: The code filters Hansard sentences, parses dependencies using spacyr to extract adjective-noun pairs and SVO triples, and calculates TF-IDF to find distinctive language.
